header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Navigating Difficult Conversations -- Feedback, Accountability, and Conflict in a Canadian Context
lesson.menu_desc: Reporting verbs e a linguagem de uma conversa difícil que a sala aguenta: blind spot, defensiveness, corrective action, to take ownership of -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 7 -- Difficult Conversations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 7 -- Difficult Conversations

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. Before your one-on-one tomorrow, you should hear this from me and not from the room. Ryan has missed three deadlines on the culture rollout. Not one. Three. And on Tuesday he told two of the coordinators that the initiative was, and I am quoting him here, imposed from Brazil. Now, here is the part I want you to sit with. When I raised it with him, he did not deny the deadlines. He explained them. He pointed out that his team was already running two accreditation audits, which is true. He insisted that nobody had asked him whether the timeline was realistic, which is also true. And then he apologized for the tone of his email, and not for one single thing that was in it. So what you are walking into tomorrow is not a man who missed a deadline. It is a man who is already defending himself, before you have said anything at all. Do not sugarcoat this, Danielle. This faculty can survive a hard message. What it cannot survive is a soft one that takes nine minutes to arrive. Name the pattern, put the three dates on the table, and then be quiet for long enough that he tells you the thing you do not know yet. I will want a debrief on Thursday. What he acknowledged, what he denied, and what he agreed to do.

Claire, it is Ryan. I have the invitation for tomorrow, and I would rather say this now than say it in a room where I am going to sound like a man making excuses. I know how the numbers look. Three dates, three misses. I am not going to argue with a spreadsheet. But I want it on the record that nobody asked me whether that timeline was possible before it was sent to me. It arrived finished. And when I raised it in March, I was told the rollout had already been approved in Sao Paulo. So I did what I could with two accreditation audits running in the same quarter, and now I am the coordinator who does not believe in the culture project. I do believe in it. I have said so in writing, twice. What I do not believe in is a plan that was built without a single question to the people who have to run it. Anyway. I will be there tomorrow and I will listen. I would only ask that she arrives with something other than a slide about ownership.

items.situation: "Look, I know the dates slipped. But nobody asked me whether that timeline was realistic."
tips: You are right, and I am not going to argue with that. What I have noticed is a behavioral pattern, and the pattern is what I want to talk about: three dates, three misses. Tell me what I do not know yet.
concede the true part -- then name the pattern anyway

items.situation: "So this is a warning. You are building a case against me."
tips: It is not the behavior itself that concerns me. It is the impact on a team that has now stopped asking you for dates. This is a conversation, and it ends with one corrective action that you and I agree on.
name the impact, not the character -- and give the exit

items.situation: "Everyone else is late too. Why am I the one in this meeting?"
tips: Because you are the one running the rollout. I am not asking you to be perfect. I am asking you to take ownership of the outcome rather than to account for who else was involved.
ownership -- refuse the comparison, keep the person

items.situation: "Fine. What do you want me to do?"
tips: One corrective action, with a date on it, that you choose and I sign off on. And then you follow through on it, because a conversation without a date is a conversation we will have again in six weeks.
corrective action + date -- never leave the room without both

items.situation: Your VP asks on Thursday: "So how did it go with Ryan?"
tips: He denied nothing. He acknowledged that the deadlines had slipped, he pointed out that his team was running two audits, and he agreed to follow through on a revised plan by the fifteenth. He also apologized for the tone of the email.
the debrief: acknowledged / denied / agreed to -- three reporting verbs, one sentence each

items.situation: "Honestly, I think head office does not trust us."
tips: Then let us address that head-on, because it is too important to leave in a corridor. What I would rather not do is sugarcoat it and have you leave this call believing that I heard you when I did not.
address head-on -- the elephant gets named, not managed
